properties.name: Mewar School (Udaipur)
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajput painting canon

facts: Earliest and most influential Rajput painting school — the reference from which every other Rajasthani school takes its bearings.
Peaked 1605-1680 under Rana Amar Singh I, Karan Singh and Jagat Singh I; the master Sahibdin painted the 1652 Ramayana of Jagat Singh (British Library).
Signature: saturated flat colour (deep reds, ochres and lapis blues), direct emotional intensity, minimal shading.
Favoured themes: Ragamala sets, Bhagavata Purana, Gita Govinda, Krishna Leela.
Sahibdin and Manohar are the two master painters most associated with mid-17th c. Mewar.

properties.name: Marwar School (Jodhpur)
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajput painting canon

facts: Bold outlines, wide flat areas of colour, and folk-flavoured energy — the least Mughal-influenced of the major Rajput schools.
Peaked under Maharaja Man Singh (r. 1803-1843), a devout follower of the Nath sect — Nath-guru portraits and Siddha-charitas dominate his commissions.
Signature palette: intense ochre-yellow ground, magenta drapery, ivory faces with sharply-drawn profiles.
Marwar sub-schools: Nagaur (softer), Ghanerao and Pali (bolder folk expression).
Bhim Sen and Amar Das were the leading atelier painters in Man Singh's Jodhpur court.

properties.name: Kishangarh School
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajput painting canon

facts: Home of India's most-loved miniature — 'Bani-Thani' (c. 1750), often called the 'Indian Mona Lisa' and issued on an Indian postage stamp in 1973.
Developed under Raja Sawant Singh (r. 1748-1764), a Krishna-devotee poet who wrote under the pen-name 'Nagari Das'.
Bani-Thani was a real courtesan-poet at Sawant Singh's court; the master painter Nihal Chand idealised her features.
Signature: elongated faces, arched brows, half-closed almond eyes, pointed nose and chin, lotus-petal lips — a stylised Krishna–Radha ideal.
Almost entirely devoted to Radha–Krishna themes set against lush Kishangarh lakes and moonlit skies.

properties.name: Bundi School
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajput painting canon

facts: Founded from the 1591 Chunar Ragamala — a dispersed set painted by Mughal-trained artists that became the DNA of both Bundi and Kota schools.
Signature: rich vegetation (banana, mango, tamarind), dark night skies, luminous white palaces, and a distinctive round-faced female type with fish-shaped eyes.
Themes favoured Baramasa (12-month seasonal), Ragamala, Rasikapriya and elephant-and-tiger hunts.
Chitrashala within Taragarh Fort — an open-corridor gallery — holds the largest surviving in-situ display of Bundi murals.
Rao Chattar Sal (r. 1631-58) and Rao Ummed Singh (r. 1749-70) were the school's leading patrons.

properties.name: Kota School
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajput painting canon

facts: Sister school of Bundi — shares the same DNA but specialised in hunt-and-jungle scenes with dense, energetic foliage.
Split from Bundi after Kota state was separated in 1631 under Rao Madho Singh.
Signature: over-life-size tiger and boar hunts, dense forest with tangled trees, action-drawing at a scale unusual for miniature painting.
Peaked under Maharao Umed Singh I (r. 1770-1819) — a passionate hunter whose personal hunts fill hundreds of surviving Kota paintings.
The famous 'Elephant Fight' and 'Tiger Hunt' at Bundi Palace are Kota-style compositions.

properties.name: Jaipur (Dhundhar) School
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajput painting canon

facts: Most Mughal-influenced of the Rajput schools — inherited direct patronage from Mughal ateliers via the Kachhawaha family's long service in Delhi.
Peaked under Sawai Pratap Singh (r. 1778-1803), who set up the 'Suratkhana' — the royal painting workshop that employed 50+ artists.
Specialised in large-scale life-size portraiture, Krishna-Leela sets, Ragamala and Baramasa themes.
Signature: refined Mughal-style drawing, sober palette dominated by ivory and mauve, meticulous jewellery detail.
Also known as 'Dhundhar' school after the historic Dhundhar region name for the Jaipur territory.

properties.name: Bikaner School
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajput painting canon

facts: The most technically refined Rajput school — Bikaner artists frequently signed and dated their work (rare among Rajasthani ateliers).
Founded when Raja Rai Singh (r. 1571-1611) brought Mughal-trained Usta-community painters from Delhi and Agra to Junagarh Fort.
Signature: pale ivory skin tones, lyrical lines, cool Persian palette (mauve, silver-grey, sap-green) — very different from the ochre-dominated Marwar palette next door.
Ali Raza, Ruknuddin and Nathu were the school's most celebrated masters — three generations of the same family across the 17-18th c.
Overlaps with the 'Bikaner Usta Art' craft tradition — the same community also created gold-manauti painted camel-hide manuscripts.

properties.name: Alwar School
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajput painting canon

facts: The last major Rajput painting school to emerge — bloomed in the 19th c. after Alwar became a princely state (1770).
Peaked under Maharaja Vinay Singh (r. 1815-57) and Maharaja Sheodan Singh (r. 1857-74).
Signature technique: paintings on ivory tablets and mother-of-pearl — a departure from paper miniatures.
Themes: Persian-inspired 'Yogini' and 'Kavi-Priya' (poet-and-lover) series, portrait sets, and detailed erotic manuscripts.
Ghulam Ali, Baldev, Buddha Ram and Chotu Ram are the main Alwar-court painters recorded in surviving colophons.

properties.name: Nathdwara School (Pichwai)
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Nathdwara Adi-Gaur painter archives

facts: Devotional-cloth painting school built around the Shrinathji temple, established at Nathdwara in 1672 after the deity's flight from Braj.
'Pichwai' means 'behind' — the large cloth hangings placed behind the Shrinathji idol to depict the season or festival.
24 seasonal Pichwai themes rotate through the temple's ritual year — from Sharad Purnima (moonlight cows) to Annakut (mountain of food offerings).
Practised by the Adi-Gaur community of painter families — many trace unbroken lineage back to the Braj artists who arrived with the deity.
Distinctive palette: rich blacks, deep greens, gold-leaf highlights and lotus-pink for Shrinathji's ceremonial garments.
